title: Chandragupta Raises the Peacock Standard
subtitle: From Rebel Challenger to Lord of Pataliputra
era: Founding of the Maurya Empire, c. 321-297 BCE
region: Magadha, Pataliputra, Punjab and the Gangetic plain
moral: A new order can rise when courage is joined to discipline.

sources.note: Greek testimony establishing Chandragupta's historical reality outside later Indian legends.
Literary drama of the Nanda overthrow and Chanakya's plotting; useful but late and legendary.

sources.citation: Upinder Singh, A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India (2008); Romila Thapar, Early India (2002)
sources.note: Modern synthesis treating his existence as secure and his origin stories as uncertain.

panels.shot: low-angle heroic wide of a young Chandragupta on a hill overlooking the Gangetic plain

dialogue.text: In the age when Magadha’s Nandas ruled from mighty Pataliputra, a young war-leader named Chandragupta rose to bind the plains under the Peacock Throne.

panels.shot: wide establishing shot of the Gangetic plain and Pataliputra’s fortifications

dialogue.text: In the fourth century before the Common Era, Magadha’s power rose on the eastern plains, its capital Pataliputra a long wooden fortress hugging the Ganga.

panels.shot: interior wide of a Nanda court scene, seen from the back of the hall

dialogue.text: The Nandas commanded wealth and elephants, and many smaller rulers bowed to their court, yet their riches bred fear as much as loyalty.

panels.shot: medium shot of a young Chandragupta in a modest rural setting

dialogue.text: Later traditions place Chandragupta’s boyhood in modest surroundings, a youth of uncertain lineage, shaped more by hard soil and drill than by palace life.

panels.shot: close-up on Chandragupta’s face as he listens to travellers

dialogue.text: By the fire he heard of Magadhan taxes, of Nanda hoards, of men crushed between greedy rulers and marching armies, and his anger grew quiet and cold.

panels.shot: medium shot in a small gathering of armed followers in a grove

dialogue.text: He drifted into the world of war-bands and clans, where strong hands and clear plans drew followers, and his tongue learned to promise a different order.

panels.shot: dramatic low-angle of Chandragupta turned away from Pataliputra in frustration

dialogue.text: Traditions remember him first rebuffed by power, kept at the gate of courts and camps, his ambitions larger than the petty commands offered to him.

panels.shot: wide shot of a northern landscape with distant foreign-style encampment

dialogue.text: To the northwest, beyond the Indus, another force had shaken the land: Alexander’s Macedonian armies, whose march stirred both awe and resistance.

panels.shot: close-up on Chandragupta in conversation with a Greek soldier in a market lane

dialogue.text: Greek writers later recalled an Indian youth, Androcottus, who moved among their garrisons, weighing the strength and limits of these new arrivals.

panels.shot: medium shot of Chandragupta addressing his men at night around a fire

dialogue.text: Magadha is rich but brittle. Strike the heart, and the limbs will fall.

panels.shot: bird’s-eye view of scattered skirmishes in the countryside

dialogue.text: He did not rush Pataliputra. He first cut at its reach, testing Nanda garrisons, loosening the grip of the king on distant lands.

panels.shot: wide shot of Chandragupta negotiating with a regional ruler in a tent

dialogue.text: Around him, minor rulers and chiefs judged the winds. Some he humbled in battle, others he bound with terms that promised order instead of chaos.

panels.shot: medium shot of Chandragupta in counsel with strategists, scrolls and maps around

dialogue.text: Indian tradition later gave this cunning a single name, Chanakya, yet no court record survives; what remains clear is that Chandragupta’s campaigns were guided by careful calculation.

panels.shot: epic wide shot of Chandragupta’s army on the march toward Pataliputra

dialogue.text: When his base was firm and his enemies divided, he turned the full weight of his army east, toward the long wooden walls of Pataliputra.

panels.shot: low-angle shot of Chandragupta before Pataliputra’s walls in battle formation

dialogue.text: Hold the lines. Break their will, and the city will open.

panels.shot: dynamic wide shot hinting at siege and maneuver without gore

dialogue.text: No detailed chronicle describes the fall, only that the Nanda king yielded and fled, and Magadha’s heart passed into Chandragupta’s hands.

panels.shot: interior wide of Chandragupta entering the Nanda palace

dialogue.text: In Pataliputra’s halls, where Nanda wealth had glittered, a new master walked, and the city’s scribes bent their records to a fresh name.

panels.shot: symbolic medium shot of Chandragupta adopting the peacock standard

dialogue.text: Later memory linked his house to the peacock, Maurya to Moriya, though the exact path of that symbol lies in layered tradition rather than clear record.

panels.shot: expansive bird’s-eye shot of the Mauryan realm radiating from Pataliputra

dialogue.text: From his new capital he spread his rule over Magadha and beyond, forging one of the subcontinent’s first great empires from the Indus to the eastern seas.

panels.shot: medium shot of Chandragupta in later years conferring with an envoy in Greek dress

dialogue.text: Greek accounts remembered him again as the Indian king who faced Seleucus, trading territory and elephants, and fixing a new balance between western and eastern powers.

panels.shot: contemplative medium shot of Chandragupta looking out over Pataliputra at sunset

dialogue.text: Behind the legends, what remains certain is this: a determined war-leader seized Magadha, raised a new standard, and showed how courage bound to discipline could remake the map of an age.
